stories.body: Israel and Iran exchanged direct strikes for the first time since an April ceasefire, raising fears that the regional war could resume at full scale. Iran fired missiles at northern Israel after Israeli strikes on Beirut, and Israel responded with attacks on Iranian military and defense sites, including a petrochemical complex, despite President Donald Trump publicly urging both sides to stop shooting and reportedly pressing Benjamin Netanyahu not to escalate. Iran said it would halt attacks unless Israel renewed hostilities, while Israel said it had paused strikes on Iran but would continue operations against Hezbollah in Lebanon.
stories.headline: Israel and Iran trade strikes as fragile ceasefire frays, with Trump urging restraint

stories.body: Iran is making any broader settlement with Washington contingent on ending Israel’s operations in Lebanon and securing an Israeli withdrawal from territory held there, tying the fate of Hezbollah to talks over the war and the Strait of Hormuz blockade. Tehran has reaffirmed support for Hezbollah, warned that attacks on Beirut cross a red line, and argued that no peace deal can restart normal shipping through Hormuz unless the Lebanon front is addressed. The stance complicates U.S.-Iran negotiations as shipping remains severely disrupted, U.S. forces strike Iranian coastal radar sites, and regional flare-ups continue despite U.S.-arranged ceasefires.
stories.headline: Iran links wider peace deal to Lebanon and Hezbollah as Strait of Hormuz crisis continues

stories.body: A magnitude 7.8 earthquake struck off Mindanao in the southern Philippines, killing at least 32 people, injuring more than 100, damaging buildings and triggering tsunami alerts across the region before most were canceled. General Santos and Sarangani province were among the hardest-hit areas, with reports of collapsed buildings, landslides, power outages and evacuations of residents and hospital patients. Authorities recorded more than 130 aftershocks and warned people not to enter damaged structures, while President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. said emergency agencies were coordinating the response and suspended classes in affected areas.
stories.headline: Powerful earthquake in southern Philippines kills dozens and triggers tsunami warnings

stories.body: Xi Jinping arrived in Pyongyang for his first visit to North Korea in nearly seven years, seeking to refresh China’s ties with Kim Jong-un as Pyongyang’s relationship with Russia has grown closer. Xi was greeted with a red-carpet ceremony, military honors and large crowds before talks focused on trade, border reopening, transport links and diplomatic and military cooperation. China remains North Korea’s main political and economic partner and its only formal treaty ally, but the visit comes amid tensions over North Korea’s nuclear program, sanctions, and Moscow’s increasingly prominent role in Pyongyang’s security strategy.
stories.headline: Xi Jinping visits Pyongyang to strengthen China–North Korea ties

stories.body: Armenian Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan’s Civil Contract party won parliamentary elections with just under 50% of the vote, strengthening his mandate to deepen ties with Europe and pursue peace with Azerbaijan after Armenia’s 2023 defeat in Nagorno-Karabakh. The result was welcomed by European leaders and viewed with concern in Moscow, which had warned Armenia against moving toward the EU and recently imposed trade restrictions on Armenian goods. Pashinyan said Armenia would continue rapprochement with the West while remaining in the Russia-led Eurasian Economic Union, though he fell short of the supermajority needed for constitutional changes tied to a final peace deal.
stories.headline: Armenia’s pro-Europe government wins election despite Russian pressure

stories.body: The Trump administration’s immigration agenda is facing simultaneous fights in Congress, the courts and oversight agencies. Senate Republicans passed a bill providing $70 billion in additional immigration enforcement funding while failing to permanently block a controversial $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization” fund for Trump allies, exposing divisions inside the party. Separately, a federal judge struck down a policy that had stalled immigration applications from people from 39 countries, a Treasury watchdog flagged security and matching problems in an IRS-ICE taxpayer data-sharing deal, and another judge halted administration conditions on SNAP funding tied to immigration and other policy issues.
stories.headline: Trump administration immigration push faces court, watchdog and funding battles

stories.body: A new AP-NORC poll finds that Americans are less likely than in recent years to see democratic government as central to the country’s identity, even as the United States prepares to mark its 250th anniversary. About two-thirds of adults now say a democratically elected government is extremely or very important to U.S. identity, down from 80% in 2021. The poll also found declining views of American exceptionalism, with only about one-quarter saying the U.S. stands above all other countries and about three in ten saying there are better countries.
stories.headline: New poll finds fewer Americans see democracy as central to national identity

stories.body: A federal appeals court ruling restricting mail-order access to mifepristone, one of the most common abortion methods in the United States, has pushed abortion policy back to the Supreme Court and into midterm campaign politics. The 5th Circuit ordered that the drug be distributed only in person and at clinics, siding with Louisiana’s argument that mailed abortion pills undermine its abortion ban, before the Supreme Court temporarily restored broader access while it considers the case. Abortion rights groups say the case could energize Democratic voters, while anti-abortion advocates are pressuring Republicans to take a harder line on medication abortion.
stories.headline: Abortion pill rulings return access fight to Supreme Court and midterm politics

stories.body: Pope Leo XIV delivered a historic address to Spain’s Congress in a joint session with the Senate, calling on political leaders to defend human dignity, lower the temperature of public debate and avoid the permanent disqualification of opponents. In a 30-minute speech followed by a long ovation, he argued that laws must be grounded in principles beyond shifting majorities, criticized discrimination against migrants, defended life “from conception to natural death,” and urged dialogue over rearmament in international conflicts. His remarks drew different points of approval across the political spectrum, with the left responding to his language on migrants and peace and the right to his statements on life and education.
stories.headline: Pope Leo XIV makes historic Spain address, criticizing polarization, discrimination and war

stories.body: Pope Leo XIV used his visit to the Spanish Episcopal Conference to describe clergy sexual abuse as a “plague” and call for truth, justice, repair, prevention and a stronger culture of care. He said people harmed by those who should have protected them must receive sincere listening, welcome, protection and real paths toward healing. Later, he met privately for nearly an hour with six abuse survivors at the apostolic nunciature, while some victim associations criticized being excluded from the encounter and Spanish officials pointed to growing use of a state-backed reparations office.
stories.headline: Pope calls clergy abuse a ‘plague’ and urges Spanish Church toward truth, justice and repair

stories.body: Spanish corruption investigations widened as National Court judge Santiago Pedraz summoned PSOE president Cristina Narbona as a witness in the Leire Díez case and formally placed Koldo García’s lawyer, Leticia de la Hoz, under investigation over an alleged bribery attempt connected to testimony about money delivered to PSOE headquarters. Pedraz also ordered testimony from more than 20 witnesses and requested information from the attorney general’s office about meetings involving people under investigation. Separately, a Madrid judge admitted PP and Vox complaints over the public rescue of Air Europa, summoned SEPI vice president Bartolomé Lora as a suspect in a possible administrative misconduct case, and demanded extensive documentation on the nearly €500 million bailout.
stories.headline: Spain’s corruption probes widen as judges summon PSOE figures and Air Europa officials

stories.body: Barcelona is preparing for Pope Leo XIV’s June 9–10 visit with major mobility restrictions around Montjuïc, the Cathedral, Sant Agustí in the Raval and especially the Sagrada Família, where the pope is expected to bless the tower of Jesus. The city is urging residents to plan ahead and use reinforced public transport, while some metro access and parking will be restricted and broad traffic cuts will affect key Eixample streets. Betevé and the city will install giant screens at Glòries and Arc de Triomf to broadcast events, while many shops and restaurants near the Sagrada Família plan to close because of access limits, terrace removals and uncertainty over the day’s operations.
stories.headline: Barcelona braces for Pope Leo XIV visit with major traffic cuts, giant screens and business closures

stories.body: Barcelona library workers continued an indefinite strike and occupied the city’s library consortium offices for several hours after holding an assembly in Plaça Sant Jaume to demand renewed negotiations with the city. Workers say the administration closed talks without accepting key demands, including salary alignment with other municipal workers, shorter workdays, better Saturday compensation, reinforced staffing and updated public library standards. The consortium says negotiations remain open and that worker representatives did not sign the administration’s proposed agreement, while the strike is continuing during university entrance exams and alongside planned protests during the pope’s arrival.
stories.headline: Barcelona library workers occupy consortium offices as indefinite strike continues

stories.body: A man was shot dead Sunday night on Carrer de la Mineria in Barcelona’s Zona Franca, the second fatal shooting on the same street in less than a month and part of a wider rise in gun violence in Catalunya. Mossos d’Esquadra are investigating the killing and searching for the attacker, while city officials said the deaths appear linked to international organized crime rather than neighborhood dynamics. Interior minister Núria Parlon called for tougher penalties for illegal firearm possession, noting concerns that drug-trafficking groups and other criminal networks have become increasingly armed, and police have reinforced their presence in the area ahead of neighborhood festivities.
stories.headline: Second fatal Zona Franca shooting in weeks raises organized-crime concerns in Barcelona
